---
title: "KV Cache Quantization Explained"
---


## What "Quantization" Means in General

In machine learning, **quantization** = representing numbers with fewer bits. Instead of storing each number as a high-precision floating-point value, we approximate it with a lower-precision format.

```
FP32  (32 bits) ──► 3.14159265358979...    high precision, big memory
FP16  (16 bits) ──► 3.1416                 half the memory
FP8   ( 8 bits) ──► 3.14                   quarter
INT8  ( 8 bits) ──► 50  (scaled integer)   quarter
INT4  ( 4 bits) ──► 3   (scaled integer)   eighth
```

It's analogous to JPEG compression for images: you trade some fidelity for a much smaller file.

## Applied to the KV Cache

Recall from before — the KV cache stores the K and V tensors for every past token, every layer, every attention head. By default, those tensors are stored in the same precision the model runs in: usually **FP16** or **BF16** (16-bit floats).

**KV cache quantization** stores those K/V tensors in a lower-precision format to save VRAM:

```
                  Memory footprint per K/V value
                  ─────────────────────────────────
  FP16 / BF16  ──► ████████████████  16 bits  (baseline, 100%)
  FP8          ──► ████████           8 bits  (50% smaller)
  INT8         ──► ████████           8 bits  (50% smaller)
  INT4         ──► ████               4 bits  (75% smaller)
```

So if your KV cache was eating 8 GB of VRAM at FP16, switching to INT4 would bring it down to about **2 GB** — freeing up 6 GB for longer contexts, larger batch sizes, or just letting the model fit on your GPU at all.

## How It Actually Works

When you store a tensor in INT8 or INT4, you can't just truncate the float — you'd lose all the magnitude information. Instead, quantization uses a **scale factor** (and sometimes a zero-point) to map a range of float values onto the small integer range:

```
  Original FP16 values in a block:
  [-2.3, -0.8, 0.1, 1.5, 2.7, ...]
                    │
                    │  find min/max, compute scale
                    ▼
  scale = (max - min) / (2ᴺ - 1)     ← N = 8 for INT8, 4 for INT4
  
  Stored as:
  ┌──────────────────────────┐
  │ scale (FP16)             │  ← one scale per "block" of values
  │ zero_point (optional)    │
  │ [int_0, int_1, int_2,...]│  ← the quantized integers
  └──────────────────────────┘
  
  Dequantize on-the-fly during attention:
  float_value ≈ (int_value - zero_point) × scale
```

The scale is shared across a **block** (e.g., 32 or 128 values), so the per-value overhead is tiny. This is called **block-wise quantization**, and it's what makes INT4 viable — without blocks, a single outlier would ruin the precision for the whole tensor.

## The Format Differences

```
┌──────────┬─────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Format   │ Bits    │ Character                                │
├──────────┼─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ FP16     │ 16      │ Float, wide range, default for most LLMs │
│ BF16     │ 16      │ Float, same range as FP32, less mantissa │
│ FP8      │  8      │ Float, preserves dynamic range better    │
│ INT8     │  8      │ Integer + scale, simpler & well-supported│
│ INT4     │  4      │ Integer + scale, aggressive, lossy       │
└──────────┴─────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**FP8 vs INT8** — both are 8 bits, but FP8 keeps a small exponent so it handles outliers better. INT8 is uniform across its range. For KV caches, FP8 is often slightly higher quality; INT8 is more universally supported in hardware/kernels.

**INT4 (Experimental)** — labeled experimental in your UI because 4 bits is *aggressive*. You can see noticeable quality degradation, especially on long contexts (errors compound across many tokens), reasoning tasks, and code generation. Some heads/layers tolerate it fine; others don't.

## The Trade-off Curve

```
  Quality
    ▲
    │  ●  FP16/BF16   (baseline, what the model was trained at)
    │   ╲
    │    ●  FP8       (tiny quality loss, ~free)
    │     ╲
    │      ●  INT8    (small quality loss, usually fine)
    │       ╲
    │        ╲
    │         ●  INT4 (visible quality loss, especially long ctx)
    │
    └─────────────────────────────► VRAM saved
       0%       50%        75%
```

The general guidance:

- **FP16/BF16** — use when you have VRAM headroom and want maximum quality.
- **FP8** — excellent default if your GPU supports it (Hopper/Ada, RTX 40-series, H100). Near-lossless in practice.
- **INT8** — broader hardware support, very safe quality-wise.
- **INT4** — only when you're desperate for VRAM (e.g., trying to run a 70B model with 32k context on a single consumer GPU). Test the output on your actual workload before trusting it.

## Why It's Specifically Useful for Long Contexts

The UI hint says "*especially for long sequences*" — here's why. KV cache size scales **linearly with sequence length**, while model weights are fixed. So:

```
  Short context (2k tokens):
  ┌─────────────────────────────┐
  │ Model weights      14 GB    │ ◄── dominates
  │ KV cache            1 GB    │
  └─────────────────────────────┘
  Quantizing KV cache → saves ~0.5 GB. Meh.

  Long context (100k tokens):
  ┌─────────────────────────────┐
  │ Model weights      14 GB    │
  │ KV cache           25 GB    │ ◄── dominates!
  └─────────────────────────────┘
  Quantizing KV cache to INT4 → saves ~19 GB. Huge.
```

For your agentic AI work — where contexts get long fast (tool definitions, retrieved documents, multi-turn history) — KV cache quantization is one of the highest-leverage knobs you can turn on a local setup.

## My Practical Recommendation

For your M3 Pro with 36 GB unified memory running local models (I assume via LM Studio, Ollama, or MLX):

- Start with **FP16/BF16** as default and only change if you're hitting memory limits.
- If you need to fit longer context or a bigger model: try **FP8** first (if supported) or **INT8**. You'll likely not notice quality differences for typical chat/coding tasks.
- Treat **INT4** as a last resort, and benchmark it on a representative task before relying on it — particularly for radiology-related reasoning or anything where precision matters.
